ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Paneer Lababdar; closest parent is cottage cheese food product (FOODON_03302571).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP represents paneer as a dairy product; no specific code exists for the prepared Paneer Lababdar dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Paneer Lababdar or sufficiently close prepared paneer dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Paneer Lababdar is approximately 250 g, comprising cubed paneer pieces in a rich tomato-cream gravy, usually served alongside naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Paneer and Paneer-based curry preparations
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports nutrient values for paneer (raw) and for paneer-based curry preparations. Paneer Lababdar values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for paneer curry with cream and tomato gravy, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) for tomato-cream sauce components. The composite per-100g values reflect a typical restaurant-style preparation with approximately 40% paneer, 35% tomato-onion gravy, 15% cream/butter, and 10% spices/aromatics.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Paneer Lababdar. GI is estimated at 35 based on the GI of paneer (Indian cottage cheese, GI ~27, University of Sydney GI Database) and the tomato-cream gravy components (tomatoes GI ~15, cream GI negligible). The composite dish GI is estimated at 35 (low), reflecting the dominant low-GI paneer and cream content with modest carbohydrate from tomato and onion. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 18.0) / 100 = 6.3 (serving carbohydrate = 7.2 g/100g × 250 g = 18.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100g) and saturated fat from cream and butter
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less and request reduced cream/butter preparation, as a 250 g serving contributes approximately 775 mg sodium (about one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) due to high protein and fat content from paneer and cream
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer Lababdar has a low glycemic index (~35) and a moderate glycemic load per serving (~6.3), making it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions alongside a low-GI staple such as roti or basmati rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~7.2 g/100g) from paneer, cream, and butter contributing to LDL cholesterol risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Due to its high saturated fat content (~18 g per 250 g serving), individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (100–150 g) and prefer preparations made with reduced cream and minimal butter to lower saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Lababdar is a rich North Indian vegetarian curry made from cubed paneer (Indian cottage cheese) simmered in a velvety, spiced tomato-cream gravy. The name 'Lababdar' is derived from the Urdu word conveying richness and indulgence. The dish is prepared by first making a base of sautéed onions, ginger, garlic, and tomatoes, which are then blended into a smooth sauce and enriched with cream, butter, and aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, red chilli, and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves). Paneer cubes are added and gently simmered until they absorb the flavours of the gravy. The result is a creamy, mildly spiced, tangy-sweet curry with a soft, milky texture from the paneer. Paneer Lababdar is a staple of North Indian restaurant menus and is popular across India and in Indian diaspora communities worldwide, including Singapore. Nutritionally, it is a good source of protein (~7.8 g/100g) and calcium (~210 mg/100g) from the paneer, but is calorie-dense (~168 kcal/100g) due to its cream and butter content. Its saturated fat content (~7.2 g/100g) warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease or hypertension. The low glycemic index (~35) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in appropriate portions. It is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian, and is typically served with naan, roti, or basmati rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Paneer Lababdar?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Paneer Lababdar as a named dish exists; nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN paneer curry data and USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for paneer and tomato components. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this preparation. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this preparation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cream, butter, and paneer components and are approximate. The cuisine_tags field in the input JSON listed 'Chicken Dish' which is incorrect for Paneer Lababdar (a vegetarian paneer dish); this tag has been disregarded. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' from the input has been retained as paneer is a protein-rich dairy alternative commonly classified in this group. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.
